title: The Day Before Hell
content:
The needle went in smooth. Twenty years of practice made it that way.

Lin Mei watched the morphine drip into the IV line, her hands steady despite the blood matting her hair, the gash across her ribs, the way her left arm hung wrong at the shoulder. The warlord's men had done their work well enough. She'd still managed to drag herself to the infirmary.

"Colonel Zhang will recover," she said to no one. The tent flap hung open. Rain pattered against canvas. "His fever will break by dawn. The infection in his leg—"

The knife slid between her ribs from behind.

She knew the angle before she felt the steel. T5-T6 intercostal space. Direct path to the heart. Someone had studied anatomy.

"You've outlived your usefulness, Doctor."

Li Wei's voice. The man whose guts she'd stitched back into his belly three winters ago. The man she'd taught to suture. The man who'd called her *shifu* for six years.

Lin Mei fell forward. The IV stand clattered. Morphine pooled on the dirt floor.

"Colonel Zhang's orders," Li Wei said, and there was nothing in his voice. No regret. No anger. Just emptiness. "A new world needs new leadership. And you know too much about the old one."

She hit the ground face-first. The smell of mud and antiseptic filled her nose. Her blood spread in a dark fan beneath her chest.

*Twenty years.*

Twenty years of cutting into the living. Twenty years of pulling bullets from children's bellies, of amputating limbs with a hacksaw, of watching people die and die and die while the world burned around her. Twenty years of keeping the warlord alive.

And this was her reward.

Darkness took her. But in that darkness, she felt something strange.

Not peace. Not the void.

*Anger.*

A cold, crystalline fury that locked around her spine and pulled.

---

Her eyes snapped open.

The ceiling was white. Not canvas white. Paint white. With a water stain in the corner shaped vaguely like Hainan Island.

Lin Mei lay motionless. Her body felt strange. Wrong. Too light. The constant ache in her knees was gone. The scar tissue across her abdomen didn't pull when she breathed. Her left arm—

She lifted it. Rotated the shoulder. No grinding. No grating. No bone fragments shifting where they shouldn't.

*Impossible.*

She sat up. The room spun. A bedroom. Small. A desk with textbooks stacked neatly. A laptop she didn't recognize. Posters on the wall—medical diagrams, acupuncture points, a calendar from the China Medical University.

She looked at her hands.

Young. Smooth. No calluses. No burn scars from cauterizing wounds. No yellowed nicotine stains from the years she'd traded her surgical skills for cigarette rations.

Her heart hammered. She grabbed her phone from the nightstand.

*September 12, 2024.*

The numbers didn't change no matter how many times she blinked.

*September 12, 2024.*

The day before.

The day before the sky split open. The day before the qi storms rolled in from the Gobi Desert, turning the air to acid, driving every living thing mad. The day before the world ended.

She had one day.

Lin Mei moved on instinct. Twenty years of battlefield triage had burned away hesitation. She was on her feet before her conscious mind caught up, pulling on clothes, shoving her feet into sneakers.

The apartment was small. A one-bedroom in a Beijing suburb. Photographs on the wall showed her with parents she hadn't seen since the storms took Beijing. A diploma from Peking University, framed and hung with pride.

She grabbed a backpack from the closet. Empty. Good.

Her phone buzzed. A message from someone named "Mama": *Have you eaten? Don't forget your auntie's birthday dinner tomorrow.*

Tomorrow.

When the sky would burn.

Lin Mei typed back: *I'll be there.* A lie. But the last thing she needed was her mother calling the police for a wellness check while she was out preparing for the end of the world.

She left the apartment without looking back.

---

The pharmacy was a twenty-minute walk from her building. She knew exactly which one. She'd passed it a thousand times in her old life, back when she was just a resident, before the storms, before everything.

The bell above the door chimed as she entered.

A young woman at the counter looked up. "Can I help you?"

"Antibiotics," Lin Mei said. "The full shelf."

The woman blinked. "I'm sorry?"

Lin Mei pulled out her medical license. Still valid. Still current. In her old life, she'd learned that paperwork meant nothing after the storms. But before them? Paperwork was everything.

"I'm a doctor," she said. "There's been a contamination at the hospital. We need to stockpile. Emergency protocol."

The woman's eyes went wide. "I haven't heard anything—"

"You will." Lin Mei's voice carried the weight of twenty years of giving orders. "How fast can you fill the list I'm about to give you?"

The woman grabbed a notepad.

Lin Mei recited from memory. Amoxicillin. Ciprofloxacin. Metronidazole. Surgical gloves. Scalpels. Sutures, both dissolvable and non. Betadine. Alcohol wipes. Gauze. Bandages. The list went on.

In her old life, she'd learned the hard way that the first week after the storms was chaos. Hospitals would be overwhelmed within hours. Pharmacies would be looted by nightfall. The people who survived were the ones who prepared. And the people who thrived were the ones who knew how to treat the wounds the storms left behind.

"What about traditional medicine?" Lin Mei asked.

"The back wall. Herbal supplements."

Lin Mei walked past shelves of vitamins and protein powders to the section labeled *Chinese Herbal Medicine*. Dried ginseng. Astragalus. Goji berries. Reishi mushrooms. Things that would become useless in the new world.

But the herbs she needed weren't here.

She turned back to the counter. "Where's the nearest herb shop? The kind that sells raw ingredients."

"Dongcheng District. About forty minutes by subway."

"Good. Have the antibiotics ready by the time I get back. I'll pay cash."

The woman nodded, still looking dazed. "Doctor, what's really happening?"

Lin Mei paused at the door.

She wanted to tell her. Wanted to grab her by the shoulders and shake her and say *run, hide, stockpile, the world ends tomorrow and there's nothing you can do but prepare.*

But she knew how that would end. The woman would call the police. A psych hold. Twenty-four hours in a hospital bed while the sky burned.

"Just a precaution," Lin Mei said. "Better safe than sorry."

The bell chimed as she left.

---

The subway was crowded. Rush hour. Bodies pressed together, faces buried in phones, earbuds in. No one looked at each other.

Lin Mei watched them.

The man in the business suit, sweating through his collar, probably heading home to a wife and kids. The teenager with a schoolbag, texting furiously, probably complaining about homework. The old woman clutching a bag of groceries, gray-haired and tired, probably cooking dinner for a family that wouldn't survive the week.

In her old life, she'd watched ninety percent of Beijing die in the first month. The qi storms didn't discriminate. They killed the rich and the poor, the young and the old, the good and the evil. They killed everyone.

But not her.

Not the first time.

And not this time either.

She got off at Dongcheng and found the herb shop tucked between a noodle house and a repair shop. The sign was faded, the windows dusty, but the smell hit her before she pushed through the door. Dried roots. Bark. Mushrooms. The smell of old China.

An ancient man sat behind the counter, reading a newspaper. He looked up when she entered.

"Can I help you, little sister?"

"I need," Lin Mei said, "raw astragalus root. Fresh, not dried. Dang shen. Huang qi. And lingzhi, the wild kind, not cultivated."

The old man's eyebrows rose. "That's a specific list."

"I'm a doctor. I know what I need."

"You're a child."

Lin Mei met his eyes. "Tomorrow, you'll know I wasn't."

Something flickered in his gaze. A recognition. A shiver, maybe. He set down his newspaper and shuffled into the back room.

She waited, hands steady, heart cold.

Twenty years in hell had taught her one thing above all else: preparation was survival. And she had twenty years of preparation compressed into twenty-four hours.

The old man returned with three paper-wrapped bundles. "Two thousand yuan."

She paid without haggling. "Do you have a mortar and pestle? Good quality stone?"

He sold her that too.

She left with her purchases, the weight of them solid and real in her bag. Not enough. Not nearly enough. But a start.

The first step on a long road to surviving the end of the world.

And maybe, just maybe, to finding Li Wei before he found her.

The sun was setting as she stepped back onto the street. Golden light painted the buildings, the trees, the people hurrying home. A beautiful evening. The kind people would remember as the last good night before everything changed.

Lin Mei checked her phone.

*September 12, 2024. 6:47 PM.*

Twenty-nine hours until the first storm hit.

She had a lot of work to do.

title: The Price of Knowing
content:
Lin Mei's fingers moved on their own.

Twenty years of battlefield surgery had wired her hands to respond before her brain caught up. The scalpel blade pressed against the man's throat—not deep enough to draw blood, but close. Close enough that she felt his pulse rabbit against the steel.

"Who sent you?" Her voice came out flat. Professional.

The man's eyes went wide. He'd expected a doctor, soft hands and softer resolve. Instead he'd found someone who'd amputated limbs with a rusted saw while monsters clawed at the door.

"My—my brother," he stammered. "He's sick. Fever for three days. The hospital turned him away."

Lin Mei held still. The qi storms wouldn't hit for another twenty-three hours. Normal people didn't know anything yet. But some things were already breaking down beneath the surface—hospitals overwhelmed, supplies running thin, the first whispers of something wrong in the air.

"Where is he?"

"East district. Fourth floor, no elevator. Building 7B."

She pulled the scalpel back. The man gasped and stumbled against her doorframe. She noted the way he moved—favoring his right leg, old injury, probably a motorcycle accident that hadn't healed clean. In the wasteland, that limp would get him killed inside a week.

"What's your name?"

"Zhang Wei."

"Listen to me carefully, Zhang Wei. Your brother doesn't have a normal fever. The hospitals can't help him because they don't know what's coming. I do."

She walked to her kitchen counter and pulled out a sheet of paper. Her handwriting had always been precise—surgeon's script, each character carved with purpose. She listed seven ingredients. Most were common Chinese medicinal herbs. One was not.

"You need to find these. You have until tomorrow morning. If you can't get the last one—the qing ming root—your brother dies."

Zhang Wei took the paper with shaking hands. "How do you know? How can you possibly—"

"I'm a doctor."

She closed the door before he could ask more.

---

The lie sat heavy in her chest as she moved through her apartment, packing. A doctor. That was what she was now, what she'd always been. But doctors in the old world followed protocols, consulted textbooks, waited for test results. The woman who'd woken up in this body three hours ago was something else entirely.

She'd learned medicine the hard way—in the burned-out shell of a provincial hospital, surrounded by the dying and the damned. She'd learned that the qi that had once been humanity's lifeblood could turn toxic, could warp flesh and bone into nightmares. She'd learned that the herbs growing in contaminated soil carried power no textbook had ever documented.

And she'd learned that the people who survived weren't the strongest or the smartest.

They were the ones who saw the truth before everyone else.

Her apartment was small but well-organized. A single bedroom, a kitchen with a gas stove, a bathroom with running water that would stop working in roughly forty-eight hours. She'd earned this place through twelve years of surgical residency, three years of attending work, and a divorce that had taken half her savings.

In the wasteland, this apartment would be prime real estate. High floor, defensible position, water access through the pipes for at least another week if you knew how to scavenge.

She pulled out her phone. Twenty-two hours remaining.

The notifications told her nothing unusual—work emails, a message from her mother asking when she'd visit, a reminder about a conference she was supposed to attend next month. The world was still turning, still pretending everything was normal.

She scrolled through her contacts. Dr. Li, chief of surgery. Nurse Wang, who always brought her tea during night shifts. Chen Feng, the anesthesiologist who'd been having an affair with his intern for the past six months.

All of them would be dead within the year.

Lin Mei closed the phone and opened her medical bag.

The bag itself was standard issue—black leather, multiple compartments, worn at the edges from years of use. She'd been carrying it when she died. When the warlord's blade had found her spine, severing everything she'd worked to build.

She remembered the look in his eyes. Gratitude, at first. She'd saved his life, after all—pulled a crossbow bolt from his chest, spent three days nursing him through the infection. Then she'd seen what he did to the settlement he "liberated." The children he sold. The women he took.

She'd tried to poison him. He'd found out.

Her fingers touched the scar that should have been on her back. The skin was smooth. Unbroken. She was twenty-nine again, reborn into a body that had never known the wasteland, never performed a battlefield amputation with a hacksaw, never watched a patient die because she'd run out of antibiotics three months ago.

But her mind remembered everything.

She unzipped the bag and began to inventory its contents. Scalpels, forceps, scissors, sutures—the basics. Bandages, antiseptic, a blood pressure cuff. She'd need more. Much more.

The front door opened.

Lin Mei's hand shot to the scalpel she'd left on the counter. She hadn't heard footsteps. She hadn't heard the lock turn.

A woman stood in her doorway. Middle-aged, graying hair pulled back in a tight bun, eyes sharp behind wire-rimmed glasses. She wore a traditional white lab coat with a hospital badge that read "Dr. Hu, Chief of Infectious Disease."

"I know who you are," the woman said.

Lin Mei's grip tightened on the scalpel. "That's impressive, since I don't believe we've met."

"We haven't. Not yet." Dr. Hu stepped inside and closed the door behind her. "But I've seen you. One week from now, you'll be admitted to my ward with a fever that won't break. I'll diagnose you with something that doesn't exist in any medical textbook. And three days after that, the building will collapse."

Lin Mei stared at her.

"You're a rebirthed," Dr. Hu continued. "Same as me. We came back from the other side. Some of us remember more than others."

"How many?"

"Enough." Dr. Hu walked to the kitchen table and sat down, folding her hands in front of her. "There's a network. We've been building it for the past six months, one person at a time. Each of us woke up at a different point—some a year ago, some a few weeks. We've been preparing."

"Preparing for what?"

"For the storms. For the monsters. For the warlords who will rise from the chaos." Dr. Hu's voice hardened. "And for the ones who know too much."

Lin Mei set down the scalpel. "You're talking about the Consortium."

Dr. Hu's eyes flickered with something—surprise, or recognition. "So you do remember. The group of scientists and military leaders who took control of the wasteland. Who hoarded the clean water and the medicine and the weapons. Who decided who lived and who died."

"I remember them." Lin Mei's voice went cold. "I remember treating their wounded. I remember watching them execute a fourteen-year-old girl for stealing a loaf of bread."

"Then you understand why we need to stop them before they form."

"Stop them?" Lin Mei leaned forward. "The Consortium doesn't exist yet. The storms haven't even started. We have time to prepare, to build something better—"

"No." Dr. Hu shook her head. "You don't understand. The Consortium isn't just a group of people who came together after the apocalypse. They're the ones who caused it."

The words hung in the air.

Lin Mei felt her stomach drop. Twenty years in the wasteland. Twenty years of fighting, scavenging, bleeding. Twenty years of watching humanity tear itself apart while monsters roamed the streets.

And it had been planned.

"The qi storms," she said slowly. "They weren't natural."

"They were engineered. A military experiment gone wrong—or gone exactly as intended, depending on who you ask." Dr. Hu pulled a folded document from her coat pocket and slid it across the table. "This is the original research. The project was called 'Heaven's Gate.' Its purpose was to weaponize qi."

Lin Mei unfolded the document. Her eyes scanned the technical jargon—quantum entanglement, qi resonance frequencies, atmospheric injection protocols. The details blurred together, but the conclusion was clear.

Someone had tried to turn the fundamental energy of human life into a bomb.

And it had worked.

"The researchers," she said. "The ones who ran the project. They knew what would happen."

"Some of them. The ones at the top." Dr. Hu's expression darkened. "They had bunkers prepared. Supplies stockpiled. They planned to wait out the worst of it and then emerge to rebuild the world in their image."

"They're already here."

"Yes."

Lin Mei looked at the document again. At the names listed at the bottom—scientists, military officials, government administrators. She recognized some of them from the wasteland. The warlord who'd killed her had been one of their lieutenants, a brutal enforcer who'd carried out their orders without question.

She was going to enjoy killing him.

"If they're already here," she said, "then we don't have twenty-two hours. We have less."

"Much less." Dr. Hu stood. "The storms will hit on schedule, but the first phase has already begun. People are getting sick. The hospitals are seeing cases they can't explain. By midnight tonight, the first deaths will be reported."

"Where do I fit into this?"

"You're a surgeon. You know how to treat the qi contamination better than anyone I've met. And you survived twenty years in the wasteland—that means you know how to fight, how to scavenge, how to lead." Dr. Hu met her eyes. "We need you, Lin Mei. The network needs you."

"And if I say no?"

"Then you die. Again. And this time, there's no coming back."

Lin Mei considered the scalpel on the counter. She considered the clock on the wall, ticking down the seconds until everything she knew fell apart. She considered the man who'd come to her door, begging for help, and the brother who would die without treatment.

She considered the warlord's face, the moment before he'd killed her.

"I have conditions."

Dr. Hu raised an eyebrow. "Name them."

"I work alone. I'll coordinate with your network, but I make my own decisions about who lives and who dies."

"Acceptable."

"I need supplies. Medical equipment, weapons, transportation. I'll give you a list."

"Done."

"And I need to know everything you know about the Consortium. Every name. Every location. Every weakness."

Dr. Hu smiled—a thin, cold expression that didn't reach her eyes. "I thought you'd say that."

She reached into her coat again and pulled out a second document. This one was a photograph. A group of men and women in formal attire, standing in front of a government building. They looked like politicians, businesspeople, academics.

Lin Mei recognized one of them immediately.

The man in the center. Salt-and-pepper hair, expensive suit, confident smile. She'd seen his face in news reports, in official documents, in the records of the wasteland's ruling council.

General Zhao. The architect of Heaven's Gate. The man who'd condemned billions to death.

"You know him," Dr. Hu said.

"I know what he did."

"Then you know what we have to do." Dr. Hu walked to the door and paused. "The storms will hit at dawn. We have twelve hours to move into position

title: The Price of One Day
content:
The fluorescent lights of the municipal hospital flickered overhead, casting Lin Mei's shadow in jagged pieces across the white tile floor. She pressed the heel of her palm against her sternum, feeling the rapid thump of a heart that had stopped beating twenty years from now.

Twenty years. Twenty years of sewing flesh, of learning which herbs could slow a qi burn, of watching children starve while warlords feasted. Twenty years of knowing that the screaming she heard at night was just the world eating itself alive.

And now she was back. Day Zero. Twenty-three hours and forty-seven minutes until the first storm hit.

"You're pale as a ghost, Dr. Lin."

She looked up. Nurse Zhang stood in the doorway of the break room, her round face pinched with concern. In the old timeline, Zhang had died during the third storm surge—crushed by a collapsing overpass while trying to reach her son's school.

"Just tired," Lin Mei said. "Long shift."

"The director wants you in his office. Something about your surgery quota." Zhang lowered her voice. "He's been in a mood all morning. I think his wife left him again."

Lin Mei almost laughed. In thirty hours, Director Wang's mood would be the least of anyone's concerns. The man would be dead within the first week, trampled in the evacuation chaos.

She walked down the corridor, past patients with ordinary problems—a broken arm, a stomach virus, a woman in labor. Normal problems. Problems that could be fixed with scalpels and antibiotics and time. In twenty-four hours, none of that would matter. A qi storm would scrub the sky clean of satellites. The power grid would fail. Hospitals would become slaughterhouses.

Lin Mei stopped outside the director's office and pulled out her phone. She had twenty-three hours and forty-two minutes. There was so much to do, and she was starting with nothing but a doctor's salary and the knowledge of what was coming.

The door opened before she could knock.

"Dr. Lin. Come in."

Director Wang was a thin man with a permanent frown etched into his face. His office smelled like cheap cigarettes and expensive cologne. He gestured to the chair across from his desk, but Lin Mei didn't sit.

"I'm resigning," she said.

The frown deepened. "Excuse me?"

"Effective immediately."

"This is about the surgery quota? I told you, the board—"

"It's not about the quota." Lin Mei's voice stayed flat. Professional. "I have a family emergency. I need to leave the city."

The lie came easy. In the wasteland, lying was a survival skill. She'd learned to do it with a straight face while talking to warlords who would kill her for a single painkiller.

Director Wang leaned back in his chair, studying her. "You've been acting strange all week. The nurses have noticed. You zone out during rounds. You—"

"I know what I've been doing." Lin Mei cut him off. "I need my medical records transferred. And I need access to the pharmaceutical supply room. For personal use."

His eyes narrowed. "That's against hospital policy."

"Then override it."

"I can't just—"

Lin Mei reached into her pocket and pulled out her phone. She'd already prepared for this. She pulled up the file she'd spent the last six hours compiling—financial records, bank statements, transaction logs. She'd found them in Director Wang's unlocked computer while he was at lunch. In the old timeline, she'd never known about his embezzlement. She'd been too busy saving lives to notice the rot inside the hospital walls.

"I can," she said, turning the screen toward him. "Or I can send this to the police. Your choice."

Director Wang's face went gray. His hand trembled as he reached for his glasses. "Where did you get—"

"The supply room. In one hour. I want the door open and the cameras off." Lin Mei pocketed her phone. "Do this, and I forget I ever saw these files."

She left before he could answer.

---

The pharmaceutical supply room was a small fortress of white shelves and climate-controlled cabinets. Lin Mei moved through the aisles with practiced efficiency, her hands steady as she filled her bag.

Antibiotics. Antivirals. Painkillers. Surgical supplies. Everything she could carry without attracting attention.

She'd done this before, in a way. In the wasteland, she'd raided abandoned pharmacies, fought off scavengers for a single bottle of iodine. But those were scraps. Leftovers. This was a fully stocked hospital, untouched by chaos.

She grabbed what she needed most: raw materials. The herbs and compounds she would need to create basic qi purification pills, wound-healing salves, antidotes for the most common monster venoms. The first wave of creatures would come with the storms—qi-twisted animals, plants that had learned to hunt, humans whose bodies couldn't handle the energy and turned into something else.

Lin Mei knew how to treat them all. She'd spent twenty years learning.

Two shelves down, she found what she was really looking for. A small glass vial filled with a dark, viscous liquid. Qi-concentrated ginseng extract. The base ingredient for the most valuable medicine she knew how to make—the one that could stabilize a cultivator's core, prevent qi deviation, extend a life by decades.

In the wasteland, a single drop of this could buy a month's worth of protection. A full vial could start a war.

She tucked it into her bag, her heart pounding. This was her insurance. Her ticket to survival.

But as she turned to leave, something caught her eye. A newspaper on the counter, left by one of the technicians. The headline caught her breath.

*WEALTHY BUSINESSMAN DONATES MILLION TO CITY INFRASTRUCTURE*

Beneath the headline, a familiar face smiled up at her. Gao Feng. The warlord who would murder her. The man who would build an empire on blood and qi cores. In the old timeline, he'd started as a minor player—a rich man with connections and ambition. But he'd used the chaos of the apocalypse to rise. To conquer. To take everything.

And now she was staring at his face, twenty-four hours before the world ended.

Lin Mei's hands moved before her mind caught up. She tore the page from the newspaper, folded it, and shoved it into her pocket. She knew where he lived. She knew where he'd be tonight.

The question was: what would she do with that knowledge?

---

The sun was setting when Lin Mei left the hospital. The sky was clear, the air warm. People walked the streets with their ordinary problems, their ordinary worries. They didn't know that in less than a day, the sky would tear open and rain fire.

She walked to the bus stop, her bag heavy on her shoulder. She had most of what she needed. Enough to survive the first month, maybe longer if she was careful. But survival wasn't enough. She'd learned that lesson the hard way.

In the old timeline, she'd been a healer. She'd saved anyone who came to her door, friend or enemy, good or evil. She'd believed that life was sacred, that her duty was to preserve it.

Gao Feng had taught her the truth. Some lives weren't worth saving. Some people were cancers that needed to be cut out.

The bus arrived, and she climbed aboard. She took a seat by the window, watching the city scroll past. The lights of skyscrapers. The crowded sidewalks. The families eating dinner in restaurants, the children playing in parks.

She knew exactly which buildings would collapse in the first hour. Which roads would become death traps. Which neighborhoods would burn.

And she knew which people would betray her.

Her phone buzzed. A text from the hospital: *Director Wang says you're on leave. What happened?*

She didn't answer.

The bus stopped at her street, and she got off. Her apartment was a small place on the fifth floor—a studio with a window that looked out at the next building. She'd lived here for three years, saving lives during the day and dreaming of the dead at night.

She unlocked the door and stepped inside. The apartment was dark, quiet. Normal.

But something was wrong.

She felt it before she saw it. A presence. A weight in the air that didn't belong. Her hand went to the scalpel in her pocket, and she moved sideways, keeping her back to the wall.

"Dr. Lin." A voice from the shadows. Male. Calm. "I've been waiting for you."

The lights flicked on.

He sat in her chair, legs crossed, a smile on his face. Gao Feng. Younger than she remembered. Cleaner. His eyes were the same, though—cold and calculating, like a predator sizing up prey.

"You're hard to track down," he said. "I had to pull some strings."

Lin Mei's heart hammered, but her voice stayed steady. "What do you want?"

"I want to talk." He stood, unfolding himself from the chair like a rising shadow. "I've been watching you, Dr. Lin. You're a remarkable surgeon. But more than that—you've been acting strange. Buying supplies. Clearing your schedule. It's almost like you know something."

He stepped closer. She didn't move.

"I know a lot of things, Mr. Gao."

"Do you?" His smile widened. "Then you know what's coming. The storms. The chaos. The end of the world as we know it."

Lin Mei's blood went cold. There was no way he could know. The storms hadn't started. No one knew except—

"I've been preparing for years," he continued. "Underground bunkers. Stockpiled weapons. People who owe me favors." He stopped in front of her, close enough that she could smell his cologne. "I'm going to survive, Dr. Lin. And I'm going to thrive. But I need a good doctor."

He extended his hand.

"Join me. I'll keep you safe. Give you resources. Protection. Everything you need."

Lin Mei looked at his hand. She thought about the knife he would drive into her back twenty years from now. The way he would smile while she bled out on the wasteland floor.

She thought about all the people he would kill. All the families he would destroy. All the lives he would end.

And she smiled.

"I'll think about it," she said.

Gao Feng's eyes narrowed. He didn't believe her. He was too smart for that.

But he nodded. "I'll be in touch, Dr. Lin. Don't take too long to decide."

He walked past her, out the door, and disappeared into the night.

Lin Mei stood in her apartment, her hands shaking. She reached into her pocket and pulled out the torn newspaper page. His face stared up at her.

She had twenty-three hours. And now she knew exactly who to kill first.

title: The Price of Preparation
content:
The fluorescent lights of the underground parking garage flickered once, twice, then died.

Lin Mei stood in perfect darkness, her hand still pressed against the cold metal of the storage unit door. She counted her heartbeats. One. Two. Three. The emergency generator would kick in within thirty seconds. She'd read the building specs in her past life, memorized every contingency plan of Jiangling Tower after she'd been forced to perform surgery by candlelight during the third qi storm.

Fourteen seconds. The lights buzzed, hummed, and snapped back to life.

She didn't move.

The door in front of her was unremarkable. Gray steel, number 47 stenciled in fading white paint, a standard combination lock that had probably cost her past self three hundred yuan. She knew what sat behind it. Twenty kilos of dried ginseng roots she'd purchased through a black market contact in Yunnan. Fifteen liters of medical-grade ethanol. A stockpile of sterile bandages and surgical instruments that would make any field hospital weep with envy.

And four bricks of pure silver.

Not for currency. Silver was useless in the apocalypse. Dehydrated food was currency. Antibiotics were gold. Clean water was worth more than a person's life.

The silver was for the formation arrays.

Lin Mei's fingers found the combination lock, spun it left, right, left. The mechanism clicked. The door swung open with a groan that seemed too loud in the empty garage.

She pulled out a single headlamp from her messenger bag, strapped it on, and stepped inside.

The storage unit was exactly as she remembered it. Stacked cardboard boxes labeled "Books — Do Not Discard." A false front. Behind the boxes, the real inventory waited in vacuum-sealed bags and military-grade medical coolers.

She'd been smart in her past life. Too smart. She'd prepared for the apocalypse with the precision of the surgeon she was. But she'd made one critical error.

She'd trusted people.

Lin Mei pulled open a cooler, checked the temperature gauge. Still cold. The vaccines inside would remain viable for another six hours. She closed it, moved to the next.

Her hand stopped.

A piece of paper sat on top of the third cooler, held in place by a roll of surgical tape. No one should have been in this unit. She'd paid cash for the rental, used a false ID, paid six months in advance.

The paper was folded once. She picked it up, turned it over.

In elegant calligraphy, four characters:

*I remember you.*

The hair on her arms stood up. She turned the paper over again. Nothing else. No name, no context, no indication of who had left it or how they'd known.

She read the characters again. *Wo ji de ni.*

I remember you.

Impossible.

She'd been reborn. No one else had come back. In her twenty years in the wasteland, she'd never met another person who remembered the old world, who spoke of the time before with the specific ache of someone who had lost everything twice.

This wasn't a message from the past. This was a message from the future.

Lin Mei shoved the paper into her pocket, her mind racing through possibilities. Fellow time traveler. Someone who had died and returned like she had. A survivor from the final days of the old world who had somehow carried their memories through the qi storms.

Or a trap.

She'd learned one lesson in the wasteland that had saved her life more times than any surgical skill: trust nothing. Trust no one. The apocalypse didn't create monsters. It revealed them.

She finished loading her messenger bag with essentials — scalpels, sutures, a small vial of the concentrated ginseng extract she'd prepared in her past life and hidden in a false compartment of her apartment wall. She'd need to replicate the formula. Fast.

The qi storms would hit in eighteen hours.

She had eighteen hours to secure a base, stockpile water, and prepare the simplest formation arrays that would keep the initial wave of qin beasts away from her location. The first wave was always the weakest. The first wave was also the most deadly because no one believed it was coming.

She locked the storage unit, reset the combination, and walked back toward the elevator.

Her phone buzzed. A message from her younger brother, Lin Tao.

*Sis, why did you send me that weird address? And why did you transfer me 500,000 yuan? Did you win the lottery?*

She typed back: *Do exactly what I said. Go to that address. Pick up the package. Don't open it. Don't tell anyone. I'll explain tonight.*

Three dots appeared. Disappeared. Appeared again.

*You're scaring me.*

*Good. Be scared. Stay alive.*

She pocketed the phone and stepped into the elevator. The doors closed, and she caught her reflection in the polished steel — a woman of thirty-two with steady hands and dead eyes. She barely recognized herself. In her past life, she'd been fifty-two when she died. The face that looked back at her belonged to a stranger.

The elevator hummed upward.

She needed to find three people before the storms hit. The old apothecary who knew the mountain herbs better than anyone alive. The disgraced martial arts master who would teach her the breathing techniques that could filter qi from the air. And the child prodigy who would grow up to be the wasteland's greatest formation master — a boy who was currently eleven years old and living in an orphanage on the east side of the city.

She'd found them all in her past life, one by one, over the course of a decade. She'd watched them die, one by one, at the hands of the warlord she'd saved.

This time, she would find them before the apocalypse. This time, they would be ready.

The elevator doors opened.

Her phone buzzed again. She glanced at the screen.

Unknown number.

*You have changed the timeline. The storms will come six hours early. Prepare accordingly.*

She stared at the message, her blood cold.

Six hours. That gave her twelve hours, not eighteen.

And someone was watching her.

She stepped out of the elevator, walked across the lobby, and pushed through the glass doors into the grimy afternoon light of the city. Cars honked. Street vendors shouted. People laughed and argued and lived their last normal day without knowing it.

Lin Mei hailed a taxi.

"East district," she said. "The Phoenix Rise Orphanage. Fast as you can."

The driver looked at her in the rearview mirror. "That's a rough neighborhood, miss. You sure?"

"I'm sure."

He shrugged and pulled into traffic. Lin Mei leaned back in the cracked vinyl seat and closed her eyes. She calculated. The orphanage was forty minutes away, assuming no traffic. She'd retrieve the boy, take him to the apothecary, then find the martial arts master. Three people. Twelve hours. A city about to collapse into chaos.

Her hand brushed against the paper in her pocket.

*I remember you.*

Whoever had left that message knew her. Knew her patterns, her hiding places, her past. In the wasteland, that would have made them either a valuable ally or a death sentence.

But this wasn't the wasteland. This was Day Zero. And she had no intention of dying again.

The taxi turned a corner, and Lin Mei caught a glimpse of movement in the side mirror. A black sedan, three cars back, matching every turn they made.

She didn't react. She didn't look again. She simply reached into her bag, fingers finding the handle of a scalpel, and waited.

The boy would wait. The apothecary would wait.

First, she needed to survive the next twelve minutes.

The taxi pulled up to a red light. The black sedan stopped two cars behind.

Lin Mei smiled without warmth.

Let them follow.

She had a message to deliver — and she was very, very good at delivering messages that left no witnesses.

title: The Dead Man's Prescription
content:
The first tremor hit at 3:47 AM.

Lin Mei felt it through the bathroom floor tiles, a low vibration that traveled up through the concrete and into her bones. The military hospital's emergency generators kicked on, flooding the hallway with harsh fluorescent light. She didn't flinch. She'd felt worse. Much worse.

Twenty-seven hours until the qi storms.

The surgery had gone well. Clean. The gang leader's son would keep his hand, would keep his function, would wake up wondering why a woman he'd never met had saved his life for a debt he didn't know existed. Lin Mei stripped off her gloves, watching the water run pink down the drain.

She'd been scrubbing for three minutes when the second tremor came.

Stronger.

Glass rattled in its frame. Somewhere above, a patient screamed. Lin Mei grabbed her bag—already packed, already waiting—and stepped into the hallway just as Dr. Zhao came running, his face the color of old paper.

"We need to evacuate. The seismic sensors—"

"It's not an earthquake," Lin Mei said.

Zhao stopped. Blinked. "What?"

She could have told him the truth. Could have explained about the qi currents building beneath the city's fault lines, about the way the world's energy was about to tear itself apart. But she'd watched Zhao die in the first wave, three years from now, torn apart by a corrupted beast while trying to save a patient who was already dead.

She didn't have time to save everyone.

"The basement," she said. "Storage Room 7B. There's a reinforced shelter."

"What? How do you—"

"Go. Now. Tell no one else."

He opened his mouth to argue. Closed it. Something in her voice must have reached him—the flat certainty of someone who'd already seen the end. He turned and ran.

Lin Mei walked.

The hospital was chaos. Nurses running. Patients crying. A man in a wheelchair trying to drag himself toward the exit while his wife screamed at him to move faster. Lin Mei stepped around them, her feet finding the path through the confusion like a knife finding the gap between ribs.

She'd learned to navigate chaos. Twenty years of battlefield surgery in a world that had lost all pretense of civilization. She knew exactly where she needed to go.

The surgical wing. Fourth floor. Office of Dr. Sun Yuxuan.

The door was locked. Lin Mei didn't bother knocking. She kicked it once, twice, and the cheap wood splintered around the latch. Inside, the office smelled like old tea and newer lies.

Dr. Sun sat behind his desk, a phone pressed to his ear. He looked up when the door crashed open. His eyes went wide.

"Dr. Lin. I was just—"

"Put down the phone."

He didn't. "The hospital director is on the line. There's been a seismic event. We need to—"

Lin Mei crossed the room in four steps. She took the phone from his hand—gently, precisely, the way she might remove a scalpel from a wound—and pressed the disconnect button.

"I'm leaving the city," she said. "I need the master key to the herbal pharmacy."

Sun's face cycled through expressions like a failing light. Confusion. Fear. Something that looked almost like recognition.

"The pharmacy is restricted. You know that. Only department heads—"

"I know." Lin Mei leaned forward, her hands flat on his desk. "I also know you've been selling hospital supplies to the Red Faction for the past six months. I know you've been skimming from the surgical fund. I know about the patient who died under your knife last March, the one you blamed on the anesthesiologist."

Sun's throat clicked as he swallowed.

"How do you—"

"I'm not interested in your crimes, Dr. Sun. I'm interested in the pharmacy. Give me the key, and I'll forget everything I know."

He stared at her. A vein pulsed in his temple.

"You can't just—"

The third tremor hit.

This one was different. Deeper. Lin Mei felt it in her teeth, in the fillings from a dental surgery she'd had at age twelve, in a life she'd already lived once. The building groaned. Cracks spiderwebbed across the ceiling.

Sun grabbed his desk to steady himself. "What's happening?"

"Something worse than you can imagine." Lin Mei held out her hand. "The key. Now."

He opened his drawer. Pulled out a steel ring with two electronic cards and a physical key. His hand shook as he passed it over.

"There's a backup generator on the pharmacy's circuit. The locks will hold even if the power fails."

Lin Mei took the key. She almost said thank you. Almost.

Instead, she turned and walked out.

---

The herbal pharmacy occupied the entire west wing of the basement. Lin Mei had memorized its layout on day one of her first life, back when she'd thought knowledge would save her. She'd spent years learning the properties of every plant, every root, every rare fungus that might extend a patient's life by even an hour.

In the apocalypse, knowledge was currency. And she intended to be rich.

The card reader beeped green. The locks clicked open. Lin Mei stepped inside and breathed in the smell of a thousand dried herbs: ginseng, astragalus, reishi mushroom, goji berry, cordyceps. The air was cool and dry, circulated by climate control systems that would keep these medicines viable for years.

She pulled a duffel bag from her pack and started loading.

She worked fast. Methodical. Her hands knew what to grab without her eyes needing to confirm. Lingzhi for boosting qi circulation. Dang gui for blood purification. Huang qi for immune system fortification. She took everything that would be useful in the first months, when the qi storms would corrupt the land and make natural herbs toxic.

Behind her, someone cleared their throat.

Lin Mei didn't stop. "I don't have time for visitors, Director Chen."

The hospital director stepped into the light. He was a small man, bald, with the kind of face that had never needed to show emotion. He held a tablet in one hand and a cup of tea in the other, as if the world wasn't shaking apart around them.

"I received an interesting call," Chen said. "A man claiming to be from the Public Security Bureau. He wanted to know about Dr. Lin Mei. Whether she'd been acting strangely. Whether she'd made any unusual purchases."

Lin Mei moved to the next shelf. Red peony root. White atractylodes. She'd need both for the anti-corruption pills that would keep the qi sickness at bay.

"And what did you tell him?"

"I told him that you were one of my best surgeons. That you'd never given me any reason for concern." Chen took a sip of his tea. "But that was before I found you in a restricted pharmacy at 4 AM, filling a duffel bag with controlled substances."

Lin Mei turned to face him.

"Director Chen. I respect you. In another life, I worked under you for fifteen years. You taught me how to close a chest wound in under three minutes. You taught me that the patient always comes first."

Chen's eyebrow rose. "I don't recall having taught you that."

"Not in this life." Lin Mei set down her bag. "How much do you know about the seismic activity?"

"Enough to know it's not normal. The sensors are picking up energy signatures that don't match any known geological pattern. It's as if the earth itself is... vibrating. On a frequency we can't measure."

"In twenty-seven hours, those vibrations will intensify. They'll tear open the sky. Creatures will pour through—things that shouldn't exist, things that our science has no name for. The world as you know it will end."

Chen stared at her. His tea had gone cold in his hands.

"That's impossible."

"I spent twenty years living in the aftermath of that impossibility. I learned to heal people with nothing but herbs and a scalpel. I learned which plants could purify corrupted blood and which would kill you faster." She picked up her bag. "I'm not crazy, Director. I'm prepared."

"Prepared for what?"

"To survive. To save who I can." She slung the bag over her shoulder. "I'm leaving the city before dawn. I know a place in the mountains—an old research station that was abandoned after the last pandemic. It has its own water source. Its own power grid. It's defensible, and it's far enough from the population centers to avoid the worst of the initial chaos."

"You're asking me to believe that the world is ending."

"I'm not asking you to believe anything. I'm asking you to give me the hospital's supply of antibiotics and surgical equipment. Everything that will expire in the next six months anyway."

Chen was silent for a long moment. The lights flickered. Somewhere above, another tremor shook the building.

"Why should I trust you?"

Lin Mei reached into her pocket. Pulled out a small notebook—dog-eared, stained, filled with handwriting that had taken twenty years to perfect. She'd started writing it the moment she woke up in her old body, pouring every piece of knowledge she could remember onto the pages before she forgot.

She held it out to Chen.

"Read page seven. The entry about the lotus seed formula."

Chen took the notebook. Opened it. His eyes moved across the page, and Lin Mei watched his face change—from skepticism to confusion to something that looked almost like recognition.

"This formula doesn't exist in any medical text I've ever seen."

"It will. In about three years, a doctor in Shanghai will discover it by accident. He'll use it to save seventeen people from qi corruption. He'll become famous for it."

"You wrote this before it was discovered."

"I wrote it because I saw him use it."

Chen closed the notebook. His hand was steady, but Lin Mei saw the vein pulsing in his temple. The same vein that had pulsed in Sun's temple, in Zhao's temple, in every person she'd ever told the truth to.

"I can't give you the hospital's supplies," he said.

Lin Mei's heart dropped.

"I can't give them to you because I can't explain the paperwork. But I can give you the key to the central supply room. I can give you the code for the security cameras. And I can be in my office, reviewing patient files, for the next hour."

He pulled a key from his pocket. Pressed it into her hand.

"If you're wrong about this, Dr. Lin, I'll have no choice but to report you to the authorities."

"I'm not wrong."

"If you're right..." He paused. "If you're right, then I hope you find what you're looking for in those mountains."

He turned and walked away. His footsteps echoed down the hallway, steady and measured, the footsteps of a man who had just made a decision that would define the rest of his very short life.

Lin Mei watched him go.

Then she picked up her bag and headed for the central supply room.

---

She had six hours until dawn.

Six hours to gather supplies. Six hours to load her car. Six hours to drive to the research station before the first qi storm hit and turned the roads into killing fields.

She worked without rest. Without pause. Her body screamed at her—the surgery had taken four hours, and she hadn't slept in thirty-six—but she'd learned to ignore pain. In the wasteland, pain was a luxury. Pain meant you were still alive to feel it.

By 5:30, she had loaded twenty-seven kilograms of herbs, eighteen kilograms of antibiotics, three surgical kits, and a portable autoclave into the back of her car. By 6:00, she had driven through the city
